## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, 3 Marks]`**

Define a D flip-flop. Write its characteristic equation and explain its operation using a truth table.

**Model Answer:**

A D (Data/Delay) flip-flop is a synchronous bistable storage element that transfers the logic level at its D input to the Q output on every active clock edge. It is the most fundamental memory element used in synchronous digital design.

$$
Q^{+} = D
$$

**Truth Table:**

| CLK Edge | D | Q (next) | Operation |
|---|---|---|---|
| ↑ | 0 | 0 | Reset |
| ↑ | 1 | 1 | Set |

The D-FF eliminates the "race-around" condition of a latch by sampling D only at the clock edge, making it the building block of all registers and pipeline stages.

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, 3 Marks]`**

List the four operating modes of a JK flip-flop based on its inputs. What is its major advantage over an SR flip-flop?

**Model Answer:**

**Four modes of JK-FF:**

| J | K | Q (next) | Mode |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | Q | Hold (No change) |
| 0 | 1 | 0 | Reset |
| 1 | 0 | 1 | Set |
| 1 | 1 | $\overline{Q}$ | Toggle |

**Characteristic Equation:**

$$
Q^{+} = J \cdot \overline{Q} + \overline{K} \cdot Q
$$

**Major advantage over SR-FF:** When J=K=1, the JK-FF **toggles** its output (a valid state), whereas an SR-FF would enter the **forbidden/invalid state** when S=R=1. Hence JK-FF is a **race-free, fully-specified** version of the SR-FF.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Write the complete VHDL code (entity + architecture) to realize a **D flip-flop with asynchronous active-high reset**, using behavioural modelling style. Mention the FPGA primitive that will be inferred. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Explain the step-by-step **FPGA implementation flow** to download the VHDL code onto a Xilinx Spartan-6 / Artix-7 board. List all intermediate files generated. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) D Flip-Flop VHDL Code:**

```vhdl
library IEEE;
use IEEE.STD_LOGIC_1164.ALL;

entity d_ff is
    Port ( D     : in  STD_LOGIC;
           CLK   : in  STD_LOGIC;
           RESET : in  STD_LOGIC;
           Q     : out STD_LOGIC;
           Qbar  : out STD_LOGIC);
end d_ff;

architecture Behavioral of d_ff is
begin
    process(CLK, RESET)
    begin
        if (RESET = '1') then
            Q    <= '0';
            Qbar <= '1';
        elsif (rising_edge(CLK)) then
            Q    <= D;
            Qbar <= not D;
        end if;
    end process;
end Behavioral;
```

**Explanation:**
- The `process(CLK, RESET)` makes the block sensitive to both clock and reset.
- `RESET = '1'` is checked **outside** the clock-edge condition — this is the defining feature of **asynchronous** reset.
- `rising_edge(CLK)` ensures edge-triggered behaviour (no race conditions).
- `Q <= D` is the characteristic equation $Q^{+} = D$.

**FPGA Primitive Inferred:**
- On **Xilinx** FPGAs: **FDCE** (D Flip-Flop with Clock Enable and Asynchronous Clear) or **FDR** (with synchronous reset) depending on the reset style.
- On **Altera/Intel** FPGAs: **DFFE** (D Flip-Flop with Clock Enable).

The synthesis tool recognizes the `if-elsif` pattern and maps it directly to the dedicated hardware FF inside a Slice/CLB.

**(b) FPGA Implementation Flow:**

| Step | Tool | Action | Output File |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1. Design Entry** | Text editor / Vivado IDE | Write `d_ff.vhd` | `.vhd` file |
| **2. Functional Simulation** | Vivado Simulator / ModelSim | Apply stimulus, observe Q | `.vcd` waveform |
| **3. Synthesis** | Vivado Synthesis Engine | Convert VHDL → gate-level netlist | `.edif` / `.ngc` |
| **4. Translation** | `ngdbuild` (ISE) / `synth_design` (Vivado) | Merge netlist with constraints | `.ngd` / `.dcp` |
| **5. Mapping** | `map` (ISE) / `opt_design` (Vivado) | Map logic to FPGA Slices/CLBs | `.ncd` / mapped `.dcp` |
| **6. Place & Route** | `par` / `place_design` + `route_design` | Place FFs/LUTs, route interconnects | placed-routed `.dcp` |
| **7. Bitstream Generation** | `BitGen` / `write_bitstream` | Convert to binary config | `.bit` file |
| **8. Download** | `iMPACT` / Vivado Hardware Manager | Send bitstream to FPGA via JTAG | Configuration of FPGA |
| **9. On-board Verification** | Push buttons & LEDs | Toggle switches, observe LEDs | Functional verification |

**On-Board Verification Example (Spartan-6/Artix-7):**
- Connect one slide switch to D input → D.
- Connect push-button to CLK input → CLK.
- Connect another push-button to RESET input → RESET.
- LED1 displays Q; LED2 displays Qbar.
- On RESET press, both LEDs go to Q=0, Qbar=1 immediately.
- On CLK press, LED copies the D switch value.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Write the complete VHDL code (entity + architecture) to realize a **JK flip-flop with asynchronous reset**. Explain how the toggle condition is handled. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Design a **T flip-flop using a D flip-flop and an XOR gate** at the structural level. Provide the VHDL code (component instantiation) and explain the synthesis result. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) JK Flip-Flop VHDL Code:**

```vhdl
library IEEE;
use IEEE.STD_LOGIC_1164.ALL;

entity jk_ff is
    Port ( J     : in  STD_LOGIC;
           K     : in  STD_LOGIC;
           CLK   : in  STD_LOGIC;
           RESET : in  STD_LOGIC;
           Q     : inout STD_LOGIC;
           Qbar  : inout STD_LOGIC);
end jk_ff;

architecture Behavioral of jk_ff is
begin
    process(CLK, RESET)
    begin
        if (RESET = '1') then
            Q    <= '0';
            Qbar <= '1';
        elsif (rising_edge(CLK)) then
            case ?  -- conceptual
            if (J = '0' and K = '0') then
                Q <= Q;
            elsif (J = '0' and K = '1') then
                Q <= '0';
            elsif (J = '1' and K = '0') then
                Q <= '1';
            else
                Q <= not Q;
            end if;
        end if;
    end process;
end Behavioral;
```

**Toggle condition handling:**
- When J=K=1, the `else` branch executes `Q <= not Q`.
- Since Q is declared as `inout`, the current Q value is read, and `not Q` is the next state — this matches $Q^{+} = \overline{Q}$ in toggle mode.
- The synthesizer recognizes this pattern and creates a **D-FF with an XOR feedback** + AND-OR combinational logic for the JK equation.

**(b) T Flip-Flop from D-FF + XOR (Structural):**

```vhdl
-- XOR gate as separate component
library IEEE;
use IEEE.STD_LOGIC_1164.ALL;

entity xor2 is
    Port ( A, B : in  STD_LOGIC;
           Y    : out STD_LOGIC);
end xor2;

architecture dataflow of xor2 is
begin
    Y <= A xor B;
end dataflow;

-- T-FF top-level using D-FF + XOR
library IEEE;
use IEEE.STD_LOGIC_1164.ALL;

entity t_ff_struct is
    Port ( T     : in  STD_LOGIC;
           CLK   : in  STD_LOGIC;
           RESET : in  STD_LOGIC;
           Q     : out STD_LOGIC;
           Qbar  : out STD_LOGIC);
end t_ff_struct;

architecture structural of t_ff_struct is
    component d_ff
        Port ( D     : in  STD_LOGIC;
               CLK   : in  STD_LOGIC;
               RESET : in  STD_LOGIC;
               Q     : out STD_LOGIC;
               Qbar  : out STD_LOGIC);
    end component;

    component xor2
        Port ( A, B : in  STD_LOGIC;
               Y    : out STD_LOGIC);
    end component;

    signal xor_out : STD_LOGIC;
    signal q_int   : STD_LOGIC;
begin
    U1 : xor2 port map (A => T, B => q_int, Y => xor_out);
    U2 : d_ff port map (D => xor_out, CLK => CLK, RESET => RESET,
                        Q => q_int, Qbar => Qbar);
    Q <= q_int;
end structural;
```

**Synthesis Result:**
- The synthesis tool identifies that `T XOR Q` is the D input of the D-FF.
- It maps this to **1 LUT (for XOR) + 1 dedicated D-FF** in a single Slice.
- The hardware realization is: D-FF whose input is T ⊕ Q, exactly matching $Q^{+} = T \oplus Q$.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 
> 1. **Using `wait until CLK = '1';` instead of `process(CLK)` with `rising_edge(CLK)`** — this is **level-sensitive**, not edge-triggered, and **does NOT infer a flip-flop**. It infers a latch. ❌
> 
> 2. **Placing `if (RESET = '1')` inside the `rising_edge(CLK)` block** — this makes reset **synchronous** (acts only on clock edge), not asynchronous. Asynchronous reset MUST be **outside** the `rising_edge` check. ❌
> 
> 3. **Forgetting the `IEEE.STD_LOGIC_1164.ALL` library** — causes compilation errors and zero marks. ❌
> 
> 4. **Using `Q` as `out` in JK/T flip-flops** — since Q must be **read back** (for the toggle `Q <= not Q` operation), it MUST be declared as `inout` or use an internal signal. ❌
> 
> 5. **Not mentioning the FPGA primitive inferred** (FDCE, FDR, DFFE) in exam answers — examiners in KTU 2024 scheme expect explicit mention of synthesis result. ❌
> 
> 6. **Confusing behavioural vs structural VHDL** — the experiment is **behavioural** for D/JK/T (one process) and may use **structural** for composite designs. Mixing them carelessly loses marks. ❌
> 
> 7. **Skipping the FPGA flow explanation** — KTU 2024 places heavy weight (5–7 marks) on the **synthesis-to-bitstream pipeline**. Always mention each step. ❌

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist:**
> 
> - [x] **D-FF:** $Q^{+} = D$ — simplest, used as universal building block.
> - [x] **JK-FF:** $Q^{+} = J\overline{Q} + \overline{K}Q$ — 4 modes (hold/set/reset/toggle); race-free.
> - [x] **T-FF:** $Q^{+} = T \oplus Q$ — toggle when T=1, hold when T=0; used in counters & frequency dividers.
> - [x] **VHDL `process` block** with `rising_edge(CLK)` is mandatory to infer a **flip-flop** (not a latch).
> - [x] **Asynchronous reset** → `if (RESET = '1')` placed **before** `rising_edge(CLK)`.
> - [x] **Synchronous reset** → `if (RESET = '1')` placed **inside** `rising_edge(CLK)`.
> - [x] **`Q` port type:** Use `inout` for JK and T flip-flops (Q must be read back for toggle).
> - [x] **FPGA primitive inferred (Xilinx):** **FDCE** (D-FF with async clear).
> - [x] **JK-FF hardware reality:** No native JK-FF in FPGA — implemented as **D-FF + LUT logic**.
> - [x] **T-FF hardware reality:** Implemented as **D-FF + 1 XOR gate** in a single Slice.
> - [x] **FPGA implementation flow:** VHDL → Simulation → Synthesis → Translate → Map → Place & Route → Bitstream → JTAG Download → On-board Test.
> - [x] **Output files:** `.vhd` → `.edif/.ngc` → `.ncd/.dcp` → `.bit`.
> - [x] **Test bench** has 3 processes: clock generator, stimulus, UUT instantiation.
> - [x] **Library always:** `IEEE.STD_LOGIC_1164.ALL`.
> - [x] **Viva favourite:** "Why is `Q` declared as `inout` in JK/T flip-flop?" — because it is **read internally** for the toggle operation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Realize in VHDL a 4-bit ALU that performs basic arithmetic (addition, subtraction) and logic (and, xor) operations on 4-bit inputs and implement on FPGA. Understand the synthesis reports and perform timing and power analysis.
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEVL508 - PCEVL508
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Realize in VHDL a 4-bit ALU that performs basic arithmetic (addition, subtraction) and logic (and, xor) operations on 4-bit inputs and implement on FPGA. Understand the synthesis reports and perform timing and power analysis.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU):** A fundamental combinational digital circuit that performs arithmetic operations (such as addition and subtraction) and bitwise logic operations (such as AND, OR, XOR) on integer binary operands. In VLSI design, the ALU is realized using a Hardware Description Language (HDL) such as **VHDL** or Verilog, and is the computational core of any processor datapath.

A **4-bit ALU** operates on two 4-bit operands `A` and `B` along with a select line `sel` (or opcode) to determine the operation. The output `Y` and a status flag `Cout` (carry-out) / `Z` (zero) are produced combinationally.

For the KTU PCEVL508 experiment, the 4-bit ALU performs the following four operations:

| Opcode (sel) | Operation     | Function            |
|--------------|---------------|---------------------|
| `00`         | Addition      | `Y = A + B`         |
| `01`         | Subtraction   | `Y = A - B`         |
| `10`         | Bitwise AND   | `Y = A AND B`       |
| `11`         | Bitwise XOR   | `Y = A XOR B`       |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The experiment is implementation-oriented. Marks are awarded for **VHDL coding correctness, FPGA implementation (synthesis, place & route), synthesis report interpretation, and timing/power analysis** — not just simulation.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **smart industrial mixing machine** in a chemical plant:

- Two inlet pipes bring chemicals `A` and `B`.
- A **control panel dial** (`sel`) tells the machine *what* to do — mix them, subtract one from another, or apply a logical filter.
- The **outlet** delivers the result `Y`.
- A **warning light** (`Cout`) glows if there's an overflow.

This machine is exactly the ALU. Instead of chemicals, it processes **electrical voltage levels (0s and 1s)**. Instead of physical valves, it uses **transistor-based logic gates** mapped onto a programmable chip (FPGA).

> [!TIP]
> Think of VHDL as the **blueprint** of this machine, and the FPGA as the **factory floor** where it is physically constructed (synthesized) and wired up (place & route).

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following **bolded metrics** are essential in KTU board examinations:

- **Propagation Delay ($t_{pd}$):** The time taken for a change at the input to reflect at the output. Typically measured in **nanoseconds (ns)**.
- **Setup Time ($t_{su}$):** Minimum time before the clock edge that data must be stable. Expressed in **ns**.
- **Hold Time ($t_h$):** Minimum time after the clock edge that data must remain stable. Expressed in **ns**.
- **Clock-to-Output Delay ($t_{co}$):** Time from the active clock edge to a valid output. Measured in **ns**.
- **Slack:** Difference between required time and arrival time. **Positive slack** = timing met. **Negative slack** = timing violation.
- **Static Power ($P_{static}$):** Power consumed due to leakage current. Measured in **mW** or **µW**.
- **Dynamic Power ($P_{dynamic}$):** Power consumed due to switching activity. Given by the standard CMOS equation.
- **Maximum Operating Frequency ($F_{max}$):** Highest clock frequency at which the design meets timing, expressed in **MHz** or **GHz**.
- **Slice / LUT Utilization:** Hardware resources consumed on the FPGA, expressed in absolute count and percentage.

---

## 1.4 VHDL and FPGA — The Two Pillars of the Experiment

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **VHDL (VHSIC Hardware Description Language):** A strongly-typed, concurrent language used to model digital systems at behavioral, dataflow, and structural levels.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FPGA (Field Programmable Gate Array):** An integrated circuit consisting of an array of programmable logic blocks (CLBs / Slices), interconnect, and I/O pads, configurable via a hardware description language.

The experiment flow follows the standard **RTL → Synthesis → Implementation → Bitstream → FPGA Hardware** path used in **Xilinx Vivado** or **Intel Quartus** toolchains.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture of a 4-bit ALU

The 4-bit ALU can be decomposed into two functional blocks:

1. **Arithmetic Unit** — performs `A+B` and `A-B` using a **4-bit ripple carry adder (RCA)**.
2. **Logic Unit** — performs bitwise `AND` and `XOR` using gate-level primitives.
3. **Multiplexer Tree** — selects the final output `Y` based on the `sel` input.

### 2.1.1 Subtraction Using Two's Complement

In hardware, subtraction is implemented as:

$$A - B = A + (\overline{B} + 1) = A + \overline{B} + C_{in}$$

where `C_in = 1` selects the subtraction mode. This reuses the existing adder hardware — a classic area-optimization technique in VLSI design.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** If a question asks "How is subtraction implemented using an adder?", always write the two's complement expression above and explain the role of `C_in = 1`.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Metric | Equation | Unit | Engineering Use |
|---|------------------|----------|------|-----------------|
| 1 | Two's Complement Subtraction | $A - B = A + \overline{B} + 1$ | — | Implement subtraction using adder |
| 2 | N-bit Adder Delay (RCA) | $t_{adder} = N \cdot t_{FA}$ | ns | Ripple carry adder worst-case delay |
| 3 | Full Adder Delay (1-bit) | $t_{FA} = t_{xor} + t_{mux}$ | ns | Critical path of FA cell |
| 4 | Maximum Frequency | $F_{max} = \dfrac{1}{T_{clk}} = \dfrac{1}{t_{su} + t_{co} + t_{logic} + t_{routing}}$ | Hz | Timing closure metric |
| 5 | Slack | $Slack = T_{required} - T_{arrival}$ | ns | Positive = pass, Negative = fail |
| 6 | Dynamic Power | $P_{dynamic} = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f$ | W | Switching power consumption |
| 7 | Static Power | $P_{static} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak}$ | W | Leakage power |
| 8 | Total Power | $P_{total} = P_{dynamic} + P_{static}$ | W | Power budget metric |
| 9 | LUT Count Estimate (RCA, N-bit) | $\approx 2N \cdot (\text{FAs}) + N$ | LUTs | Resource estimation for Vivado |
| 10 | Carry Propagation Delay (MUX) | $t_{carry} = t_{mux,A} + t_{mux,B}$ | ns | Carry chain critical path |

> [!WARNING]
> In the formula sheet, never use vertical bars `|x|` directly inside markdown tables. Use `\vert x \vert` to avoid breaking the table parser.

## 2.3 Engineering Utility

The 4-bit ALU is the **pedagogical cornerstone** of all processor design:

- It is the **execution unit** of simple microcontrollers (e.g., 8051, PIC).
- It forms the **datapath core** in RISC-V, ARM Cortex-M0, and MIPS single-cycle processors.
- At the **ASIC level**, it is hardened as a standard cell in CPU IPs by ARM, Intel, AMD, and Qualcomm.
- At the **FPGA level**, the same ALU is mapped to **LUTs, carry chains, and MUX resources** on the fabric.

The skill of reading **synthesis reports** (post-synthesis) and **implementation reports** (post-place & route) is what differentiates a hardware engineer from a software coder — and this is precisely what KTU PCEVL508 tests.

## 2.4 Synthesis Report — What the Examiner Expects

> [!NOTE]
> A **synthesis report** (e.g., from Vivado) typically contains the following sections which students must understand:

1. **Primitive / Cell Usage:** Number of LUTs, FFs, MUXes, carry cells used.
2. **Timing Summary:** Worst Negative Slack (WNS), Total Negative Slack (TNS), Worst Hold Slack (WHS).
3. **Power Report:** Static power, dynamic power, and confidence level.
4. **Resource Utilization:** Slice registers, slice LUTs, bonded IOBs, DSP blocks.
5. **Netlist Information:** Hierarchical RTL structure, inferred primitives.

For a 4-bit ALU on **Xilinx Artix-7 (xc7a35tcpg236-1)**:

- **LUTs (as logic):** ~6–10
- **Carry Cells (CARRY4):** 2
- **I/O Pins:** ~13 (8 inputs A & B, 2 sel, 1 output, 1 carry-out, 1 zero flag)
- **Worst-case Delay:** ~6–9 ns
- **F_max:** ~150–200 MHz
- **Dynamic Power:** ~0.5–2 mW (at 100 MHz)
- **Static Power:** ~50–100 µW

These are typical numbers — actual figures depend on device, tool, and constraints.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Step-by-Step Derivation: 4-bit Adder Delay

A 4-bit ripple carry adder consists of **4 full adders (FA)** connected in series. The carry must ripple from the LSB (bit 0) to the MSB (bit 3).

### Step 1: Define the Full Adder Critical Path

A 1-bit full adder has the following critical path:

$$t_{FA} = t_{xor,sum} + t_{carry,gen}$$

For a CMOS implementation using a **mirror adder** cell, this delay is typically $2 \cdot t_{XOR} + t_{MUX}$.

### Step 2: Propagate Through N Stages

For an N-bit adder, the carry chain adds one FA delay per stage:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{adder}(N) &= t_{FA,0} + t_{FA,1} + t_{FA,2} + \ldots + t_{FA,N-1} \\
t_{adder}(N) &= N \cdot t_{FA} \\
t_{adder}(4) &= 4 \cdot t_{FA}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **why** behind the term "ripple carry" — the carry signal literally ripples (propagates) one FA per gate delay.

### Step 3: Compute the Maximum Frequency

The maximum clock frequency at which the ALU can be reliably clocked (assuming a registered output) is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{clk} &\geq t_{su} + t_{co} + t_{logic} + t_{routing} \\
F_{max} &= \frac{1}{T_{clk}}
\end{aligned}
$$

For the 4-bit adder path:
- $t_{su}$ (FF setup) = 0.5 ns
- $t_{co}$ (FF clk-to-out) = 0.5 ns
- $t_{logic}$ (4-bit RCA) = 4 × 0.7 ns = 2.8 ns
- $t_{routing}$ (estimated) = 1.0 ns

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{clk} &= 0.5 + 0.5 + 2.8 + 1.0 = 4.8 \text{ ns} \\
F_{max} &= \frac{1}{4.8 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 208 \text{ MHz}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> This matches typical Vivado post-implementation reports for Artix-7 FPGAs.

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation: Dynamic Power for the 4-bit ALU

The standard CMOS dynamic power equation is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{dynamic} &= \alpha \cdot C \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f \\
P_{static} &= V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak} \\
P_{total} &= P_{dynamic} + P_{static}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Worked Example: Typical 4-bit ALU on Artix-7

Given:
- $V_{DD}$ = 1.0 V (core voltage of Artix-7)
- $f$ = 100 MHz
- $\alpha$ (switching activity) = 0.1 (typical for random data)
- $C$ (load capacitance) = 50 pF (estimated total switched capacitance)
- $I_{leak}$ = 50 µA

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{dynamic} &= 0.1 \times 50 \times 10^{-12} \times 1.0^{2} \times 100 \times 10^{6} \\
P_{dynamic} &= 0.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ W} = 0.5 \text{ mW} \\
P_{static} &= 1.0 \times 50 \times 10^{-6} = 50 \text{ µW} \\
P_{total} &= 0.5 \text{ mW} + 0.05 \text{ mW} = 0.55 \text{ mW}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Dynamic power dominates at high frequencies. Reducing $V_{DD}$ is the **most effective** power-saving knob (squared relationship), followed by reducing switching activity $\alpha$ via clock gating.

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Derivation: Two's Complement Subtraction Logic

To compute $Y = A - B$ in 4 bits:

### Step 1: Take the Bitwise NOT of B

$$
\overline{B} = \overline{B[3]} \quad \overline{B[2]} \quad \overline{B[1]} \quad \overline{B[0]}
$$

### Step 2: Add A, NOT(B), and 1

$$
Y = A + \overline{B} + 1
$$

The "+1" is implemented by setting the carry-in of the LSB full adder to **1**.

### Step 3: VHDL Inference

```vhdl
case sel is
  when "01" =>  -- Subtraction mode
    Cout <= '0';  -- Overflow ignored in 4-bit
    Y    <= std_logic_vector(unsigned(A) - unsigned(B));
    -- Or: Y <= std_logic_vector(unsigned(A) + (not unsigned(B)) + 1);
  when others => ...
end case;
```

> [!TIP]
> In KTU lab exams, the **VHDL code must be synthesizable** — avoid `wait for`, file I/O, or testbench-only constructs inside the design entity.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Synthesis vs. Implementation Reports

| Metric                  | Synthesis Report    | Implementation (P&R) Report | Why It Differs                                  |
|-------------------------|---------------------|------------------------------|-------------------------------------------------|
| Delay Estimate          | Optimistic          | Realistic (post-routing)     | Routing delays not modeled in synthesis         |
| LUT Count               | Lower bound         | Slightly higher              | Logic replication by P&R to meet timing         |
| Power Estimate          | Approximate         | More accurate                | Real wire capacitances, switching activity     |
| Slack                   | Often +∞ (combinational) | Finite, positive/negative | Wires + setup/hold constraints                  |
| Resource Usage          | Generic primitives  | Device-specific (CARRY4, etc.) | FPGA primitive mapping                         |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Top-Level Block Diagram of the 4-bit ALU

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input A - 4 bits] --> MUX1
    B[Input B - 4 bits] --> MUX1
    SEL[Select Lines - 2 bits] --> MUX1
    MUX1 --> ADDER[4-bit Ripple Carry Adder]
    ADDER --> ADD_OUT[Add Result Y]
    B --> NOTB[Bitwise NOT of B]
    NOTB --> ADDER
    MUX2[Sub Mode Logic] --> ADDER
    A --> LOGIC[Logic Unit - AND and XOR]
    B --> LOGIC
    LOGIC --> LOGIC_OUT[Logic Result]
    ADD_OUT --> OUTMUX
    LOGIC_OUT --> OUTMUX
    SEL --> OUTMUX
    OUTMUX[Output Multiplexer 4x1] --> Y[Output Y - 4 bits]
    ADDER --> COUT[Carry Out Flag]
    Y --> ZFLAG[Zero Flag Generator]
    ZFLAG --> Z[Zero Flag]
```

> [!NOTE]
> This block diagram shows the **functional data flow** of the 4-bit ALU. The adder is reused for both addition and subtraction. The output MUX selects between arithmetic and logic results.

flowchart TB
    TOP[ALU_4bit - Top Level Entity]
    TOP --> DEC[Decoder sel to one-hot]
    DEC --> A1[Add Subunit A_add]
    DEC --> S1[Subtract Subunit A_sub]
    DEC --> L1[Logic AND Subunit A_and]
    DEC --> L2[Logic XOR Subunit A_xor]
    A1 --> MUX4[4x1 Output Multiplexer]
    S1 --> MUX4
    L1 --> MUX4
    L2 --> MUX4

flowchart LR
    VHDL[VHDL Source Files] --> ELAB[Elaborate RTL]

## 4.4 Carry Chain Mapped to FPGA (Artix-7 CARRY4)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FA0[FA Bit 0 - CARRY4 Slice] --> FA1[FA Bit 1 - CARRY4 Slice]
    FA1 --> FA2[FA Bit 2 - CARRY4 Slice]
    FA2 --> FA3[FA Bit 3 - CARRY4 Slice]
    FA3 --> COUT_OUT[Carry Out Cout]
    FA0 --> SUM0[Sum Bit 0]
    FA1 --> SUM1[Sum Bit 1]
    FA2 --> SUM2[Sum Bit 2]
    FA3 --> SUM3[Sum Bit 3]
```

> [!NOTE]
> Xilinx Artix-7 uses **CARRY4 primitives** — each CARRY4 contains 4 carry-chain MUXes and associated XOR gates. This is the dedicated fast-carry path that makes adders very fast on Xilinx FPGAs.

## 4.5 Operation Truth Table

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Input A and B] --> S{SEL value}
    S -->|00| ADD[Y = A plus B Cout = carry]
    S -->|01| SUB[Y = A minus B Cout = borrow as carry]
    S -->|10| AND[Y = A AND B Cout = 0]
    S -->|11| XOR[Y = A XOR B Cout = 0]
    ADD --> Z[Update Zero flag Z]
    SUB --> Z
    AND --> Z
    XOR --> Z
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define an Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU). List the operations performed by the 4-bit ALU in this experiment.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> An **Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU)** is a combinational digital circuit that performs arithmetic and logical operations on binary operands under the control of selection lines.

The 4-bit ALU designed in this experiment performs the following four operations:

| Opcode (sel) | Operation       | Result        |
|--------------|------------------|---------------|
| `00`         | Addition         | `Y = A + B`   |
| `01`         | Subtraction      | `Y = A - B`   |
| `10`         | Bitwise AND      | `Y = A AND B` |
| `11`         | Bitwise XOR      | `Y = A XOR B` |

Additionally, the ALU generates a **carry-out (Cout)** flag and a **zero (Z)** flag as status outputs.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Jun 2023]**
**What is the role of VHDL in FPGA design? Mention any two advantages of using VHDL.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **VHDL (VHSIC Hardware Description Language)** is a standardized hardware description language used to model, simulate, and synthesize digital systems onto programmable hardware like FPGAs.

**Two Advantages:**
1. **Strong Typing:** VHDL is strongly typed, catching many design errors at compile time (e.g., mismatched bit widths, type mismatches).
2. **Technology Independence:** The same VHDL code can be synthesized to multiple FPGA vendors (Xilinx, Intel, Lattice) or even to ASIC targets, without rewriting the source.
3. **Hierarchical Design:** Supports modular, hierarchical design using entities and components, improving code reusability and readability.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a)** Write the complete synthesizable **VHDL code** for a 4-bit ALU that performs addition, subtraction, AND, and XOR operations as per the table below. Use a single entity with a `case` statement. Also generate a **zero flag** `Z` which is high when the result `Y` is zero. **(7 marks)**

| sel | Operation |
|-----|-----------|
| `00` | A + B     |
| `01` | A - B     |
| `10` | A AND B   |
| `11` | A XOR B   |

**(b)** Draw the **synthesis report interpretation** showing the inferred primitives, LUT count, and maximum combinational path delay for the above design on a Xilinx Artix-7 FPGA. Explain the meaning of **Worst Negative Slack (WNS)** and **Total Negative Slack (TNS)**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.A (a)**

**VHDL Code:**

```vhdl
library IEEE;
use IEEE.STD_LOGIC_1164.ALL;
use IEEE.NUMERIC_STD.ALL;

entity ALU_4bit is
    Port ( A   : in  STD_LOGIC_VECTOR(3 downto 0);
           B   : in  STD_LOGIC_VECTOR(3 downto 0);
           sel : in  STD_LOGIC_VECTOR(1 downto 0);
           Y   : out STD_LOGIC_VECTOR(3 downto 0);
           Cout: out STD_LOGIC;
           Z   : out STD_LOGIC);
end ALU_4bit;

architecture Behavioral of ALU_4bit is
    signal result : std_logic_vector(4 downto 0);
    signal y_int  : std_logic_vector(3 downto 0);
begin
    process(A, B, sel)
    begin
        case sel is
            when "00" =>  -- Addition
                result <= ('0' & A) + ('0' & B);
            when "01" =>  -- Subtraction via two's complement
                result <= ('0' & A) - ('0' & B);
            when "10" =>  -- Bitwise AND
                result <= "0000" & (A and B);
            when "11" =>  -- Bitwise XOR
                result <= "0000" & (A xor B);
            when others =>
                result <= (others => '0');
        end case;
    end process;

    y_int <= result(3 downto 0);
    Cout  <= result(4);
    Y     <= y_int;
    Z     <= '1' when y_int = "0000" else '0';
end Behavioral;
```

**Explanation of Key Lines (worth 1 mark each in marking scheme):**
1. **Library declarations:** `STD_LOGIC_1164` and `NUMERIC_STD` for signed/unsigned arithmetic.
2. **Port declaration:** 4-bit `A`, `B`, 2-bit `sel`, 4-bit `Y`, 1-bit `Cout`, 1-bit `Z`.
3. **`case sel` statement:** implements the four operations.
4. **Two's complement subtraction:** `'0' & A` ensures 5-bit width to hold the carry.
5. **Zero flag generation:** combinational comparator against `"0000"`.

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.A (b)**

**Typical Synthesis Report Excerpt (Xilinx Artix-7, xc7a35tcpg236-1):**

```
+----------------------------+------+-------+-----------+-------+
|          Site Type         | Used | Fixed | Available | Util% |
+----------------------------+------+-------+-----------+-------+
| Slice LUTs                 |   8  |     0 |     20800 |  0.04 |
|   LUT as Logic             |   8  |     0 |     20800 |  0.04 |
|   LUT as Memory            |   0  |     0 |      9600 |  0.00 |
| Slice Registers            |   0  |     0 |     41600 |  0.00 |
| CARRY4                     |   2  |     0 |        50 |  4.00 |
| Bonded IOB                 |  14  |     0 |       106 | 13.21 |
+----------------------------+------+-------+-----------+-------+

Timing Summary:
   WNS = 5.123 ns (positive slack — timing met)
   TNS = 0.0 ns    (no negative slack paths)
   WHS = 0.342 ns  (hold slack)
   THS = 0.0 ns

Estimated Power:
   Total On-Chip Power: 0.62 mW
       - Dynamic: 0.55 mW (88%)
       - Static:  0.07 mW (12%)
   Confidence Level: Low (post-synthesis estimate)
```

**Worst Negative Slack (WNS):** The **smallest (most negative) slack** among all timing paths in the design. A **positive WNS** means all paths meet timing. A **negative WNS** indicates a timing violation on at least one path. For purely combinational designs, WNS is measured against the `create_clock` constraint or auto-derived.

**Total Negative Slack (TNS):** The **sum of all negative slacks** across every failing path in the design. **TNS = 0** means there are no failing paths. TNS indicates the *total severity* of timing violations across the design.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes Students Make:**
> 1. Confusing **WNS** with **TNS** (WNS = worst single path; TNS = sum of all bad paths).
> 2. Reporting **post-synthesis** values as final — KTU expects **post-implementation (P&R)** values.
> 3. Forgetting to specify the **target device** in the report.
> 4. Confusing **static** and **dynamic** power.

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a)** With the help of a **block diagram**, explain the internal architecture of the 4-bit ALU. Show how **subtraction is implemented using the same adder** that is used for addition. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the 4-bit ALU implemented on a Xilinx Artix-7 FPGA, the synthesis tool reports a combinational path delay of **6.5 ns**. The clock period constraint is **10 ns**. The setup time of the flip-flop is **0.5 ns** and the clock-to-output delay is **0.4 ns**. Compute the **slack**, **maximum operating frequency ($F_{max}$)**, and verify whether the design meets the timing. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.B (a)**

**Architecture Overview:**

The 4-bit ALU is built from three major blocks:
1. **4-bit Ripple Carry Adder (RCA):** Used for both `A+B` and `A-B`.
2. **Bitwise Logic Unit:** Implements AND and XOR on `A` and `B`.
3. **4-to-1 Output Multiplexer:** Selects the final `Y` based on `sel`.

**Block Diagram:**

```
A[3:0] -----+--------+--------+
            |        |        |
            v        v        |
         +-----+   +-----+    |
         | AND |   | XOR |    |
         +--+--+   +--+--+    |
            |        |        |
B[3:0] -----+--------+        |
            |        |        |
            v        v        v
        +-----------+   +-----------+
sel ---> | Output    |<--| Adder/    |
        | MUX (4x1) |   | Subtractor|
        +-----+-----+   +-----+-----+
              |               |
              v               v
             Y[3:0]         Cout
```

**Subtraction Using the Adder:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A - B &= A + (-B) \\
-B &= \overline{B} + 1 \quad \text{(two's complement)} \\
\therefore A - B &= A + \overline{B} + 1
\end{aligned}
$$

In hardware:
- **XOR gates** are placed at the `B` input of the adder. When `sub_mode = 1`, these invert `B` to $\overline{B}$.
- The **carry-in** of the LSB full adder is tied to `sub_mode`.
- The same adder hardware thus performs both `A+B` (when `sub_mode = 0`) and `A-B` (when `sub_mode = 1`).

This technique is called **adder sharing** and is a fundamental **area optimization** strategy in VLSI design.

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.B (b)**

**Given Data:**
- Combinational path delay: $t_{logic} = 6.5$ ns
- Clock period constraint: $T_{clk} = 10$ ns
- Setup time: $t_{su} = 0.5$ ns
- Clock-to-output delay: $t_{co} = 0.4$ ns

**Step 1: Compute the Required Time**

$$
T_{required} = T_{clk} - t_{su} = 10 - 0.5 = 9.5 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the Arrival Time at the Destination Flip-Flop**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{arrival} &= t_{co} + t_{logic} \\
T_{arrival} &= 0.4 + 6.5 = 6.9 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the Slack**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Slack} &= T_{required} - T_{arrival} \\
\text{Slack} &= 9.5 - 6.9 = +2.6 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

> Since **Slack is positive (+2.6 ns)**, the design **MEETS the timing constraint**.

**Step 4: Compute the Maximum Operating Frequency ($F_{max}$)**

The minimum clock period that satisfies timing is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{clk,min} &= t_{co} + t_{logic} + t_{su} \\
T_{clk,min} &= 0.4 + 6.5 + 0.5 = 7.4 \text{ ns} \\
F_{max} &= \frac{1}{T_{clk,min}} = \frac{1}{7.4 \times 10^{-9}} \\
F_{max} &\approx 135.14 \text{ MHz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:**
- Slack = **+2.6 ns** (timing met)
- $F_{max}$ ≈ **135.14 MHz**
- The design can operate at any clock frequency up to **135.14 MHz** while meeting the setup time requirement.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:**
> 1. Forgetting to subtract $t_{su}$ when computing $T_{required}$.
> 2. Confusing **arrival time** (when data reaches FF) with **required time** (when data must be stable).
> 3. Computing $F_{max}$ from $T_{clk,min}$, not from the constrained $T_{clk}$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-density revision checklist for KTU PCEVL508 Module Experiment 2:**

- [ ] A 4-bit ALU operates on two 4-bit operands `A[3:0]` and `B[3:0]` selected by a 2-bit `sel` line.
- [ ] The four operations are: `00 → A+B`, `01 → A-B`, `10 → A AND B`, `11 → A XOR B`.
- [ ] **Subtraction is implemented as $A - B = A + \overline{B} + 1$** using the same adder with `C_in = 1`.
- [ ] The design has outputs: `Y[3:0]`, `Cout` (carry), and `Z` (zero flag).
- [ ] VHDL must be **synthesizable** — use `STD_LOGIC` and `NUMERIC_STD` libraries.
- [ ] Use a `case sel` statement inside a `process(A, B, sel)` block for the combinational logic.
- [ ] On Xilinx FPGAs, adders are mapped to **CARRY4 primitives** for fast carry propagation.
- [ ] **Synthesis report** provides *estimated* LUTs, FFs, and combinational delays.
- [ ] **Implementation (P&R) report** provides *actual* post-routing delays, slack, and power.
- [ ] **WNS (Worst Negative Slack)** = most negative slack in the design. Positive = pass.
- [ ] **TNS (Total Negative Slack)** = sum of all negative slacks. Zero = no failures.
- [ ] **F_max = 1 / (t_co + t_logic + t_su)** for a single-clock-edge, register-to-register path.
- [ ] **Dynamic Power $P_{dynamic} = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f$** dominates at high frequencies.
- [ ] **Static Power $P_{static} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak}$** is temperature-dependent.
- [ ] On a Xilinx Artix-7, a 4-bit ALU typically uses **6–10 LUTs and 2 CARRY4 cells**.
- [ ] Typical **F_max** is **100–200 MHz** for a 4-bit ALU on Artix-7.
- [ ] The **bitstream file (.bit)** is generated after implementation and used to program the FPGA.
- [ ] **Vivado Design Flow:** RTL → Synthesis → Implementation → Bitstream → Hardware Test.
- [ ] When asked for **power reduction**, mention **clock gating, voltage scaling, and operand isolation**.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Tip:** When asked "interpret the synthesis report," always state:
> 1. The **target device** (e.g., xc7a35tcpg236-1).
> 2. The **resource utilization** (LUT, FF, CARRY4 counts).
> 3. The **timing metric** (WNS, TNS).
> 4. The **power estimate** (static + dynamic).
> 5. The **confidence level** of the report (Low for synthesis, High for implementation).
